analogy: Think of "Determination of Income and Employment" through the chapter's own opening images: 36 36 36 36 36 Introductory Macroeconomics Money and Banking Money and Banking Money and Banking Money and Banking Money and Banking Money is the commonly accepted medium of exchange. In an economy which consists of only one individual there cannot be any exchange of... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 3.1 FUNCTIONS OF MONEY, 3.2 DEMAND FOR MONEY AND SUPPLY OF MONEY, 3.3 MONEY CREATION BY BANKING SYSTEM, 3.3.1 Balance Sheet of a Fictional Bank. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Determination of Income and Employment is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 36 36 36 36 36 Introductory Macroeconomics Money and Banking Money and Banking Money and Banking Money and Banking Money and Banking Money is the commonly accepted medium of exchange.

The text explains that in an economy which consists of only one individual there cannot be any exchange of commodities and hence there is no role for money.

The text explains that even if there is more than one individual but these individuals do not take part in market transactions, example: family living on an isolated island, money has no function for them.

The text explains that however, as soon as there is more than one economic agent who engage themselves in transactions through the market, money becomes an important instrument for facilitating these exchanges.

The text explains that economic exchanges without the mediation of money are referred to as barter exchanges.

The text explains that however, they presume the rather improbable double coincidence of wants.

The text explains that consider, for example, an individual who has a surplus of rice which she wishes to exchange for clothing.

The text explains that if she is not lucky enough she may not be able to find another person who has the diametrically opposite demand for rice with a surplus of clothing to offer in exchange.

The text explains that the search costs may become prohibitive as the number of individuals increases.

The text explains that thus, to smoothen the transaction, an intermediate good is necessary which is acceptable to both parties.

The text explains that the individuals can then sell their produces for money and use this money to purchase the commodities they need.

The text explains that though facilitation of exchanges is considered to be the principal role of money, it serves other purposes as well.

The text explains that following are the main functions of money in a modern economy.

The text explains that 3.1 FUNCTIONS OF MONEY As explained above, the first and foremost role of money is that it acts as a medium of exchange.

The text explains that barter exchanges become extremely difficult in a large economy because of the high costs people would have to incur looking for suitable persons to exchange their surpluses.

The text explains that money also acts as a convenient unit of account.

The text explains that it is difficult to carry forward one’s wealth under the barter system.

The text explains that you may have to spend considerable time and resources looking for people with a demand for rice when you wish to exchange your stock for buying other commodities.

The text explains that some countries have made an attempt to move towards an economy which use less of cash and more of digital transactions.

The text explains that a cashless society describes an economic state whereby financial transactions are not connected with money in the form of physical bank notes or coins but rather through the transfer of digital information (usually an electronic representation of money) between the transacting parties.

The text explains that in India government has been consistently investing in various reforms for greater financial inclusion.

The text explains that during the last few years’ initiatives such as Jan Dhan accounts, Aadhar enabled payment systems, e –Wallets, National financial Switch (NFS) and others have strengthened the government resolve to go cashless.

The text explains that supply of Money In a modern economy, money comprises cash and bank deposits.

The text explains that these are created by a system comprising two types of institutions: central bank of the economy and the commercial banking system.

The text explains that central bank Central Bank is a very important institution in a modern economy.

The text explains that it is the custodian of the foreign exchange reserves of the economy.

The text explains that it also acts as a bank to the banking system, which is discussed in detail later.

The text explains that commercial Banks Commercial banks are the other type of institutions which are a part of the money-creating system of the economy.

The text explains that in the following section we look at the commercial banking system in detail.

The text explains that the process of deposit and loan (credit) creation by banks is explained below.

The text explains that in order to understand this process, let us discuss a story.

The text explains that in effect, the paper receipts, acting as money, would 1See the box on the measures of money supply at the end of the chapter.

The text explains that the modern banking system works precisely the way Lala behaves in this example.

The text explains that 3.3 MONEY CREATION BY BANKING SYSTEM Banks can create money in a manner similar to that as given in Lala’s story.

The text explains that conventionally, the assets of the firm are recorded on the left hand side and liabilities on the right hand side.

The text explains that hence if assets are greater than liabilities, they are recorded on the right hand side as Net Worth.

The text explains that 3.1 Balance Sheet of a Bank If we assume that there is no currency in circulation, then the total money supply in the economy will be equal to Rs 100.

The text explains that we can understand this by going back to our fictional example of an economy with one bank.

The text explains that the process keeps repeating itself till all the required reserves become Rs 100.

The text explains that the RBI controls the money supply in the economy in various ways.

The text explains that but the banking system would now be able to loan Rs 300 only.

The text explains that open Market Operations refers to buying and selling of bonds issued by the Government in the open market.

The text explains that this purchase and sale is entrusted to the Central bank on behalf of the Government.

The text explains that when RBI buys a Government bond in the open market, it pays for it by giving a cheque.

The text explains that this cheque increases the total amount of reserves in the economy and thus increases the money supply.

The text explains that there are two types of open market operations: outright and repo.

The text explains that outright open market operations are permanent in nature: when the central bank buys these securities (thus injecting money into the system), it is without any promise to sell them later.

The text explains that similarly, when the central bank sells these securities (thus withdrawing money from the system), it is without any promise to buy them

The text explains that how is it related to the value of transactions over a specified period of time?

The text explains that what are the alternative definitions of money supply in India?

The text explains that what determines the value of this multiplier?

The text explains that what are the instruments of monetary policy of RBI?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Introductory Macroeconomics, Chapter 3: Determination of Income and Employment

prelims_traps: Do not treat 3.1 FUNCTIONS OF MONEY and 3.2 DEMAND FOR MONEY AND SUPPLY OF MONEY as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Determination of Income and Employment; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Determination of Income and Employment through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 3.1 FUNCTIONS OF MONEY and 3.2 DEMAND FOR MONEY AND SUPPLY OF MONEY to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
